title: The burden of disease vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The burden of disease is measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs). DALYs measure the total burden of disease – both from years of life lost due to premature death and years lived with a disability. One DALY equals one lost year of healthy life. Here it is age-standardized and expressed per 100,000 individuals.
caption: Across countries in 2023, the burden of disease measured in Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALYs) per 100,000 people shows a strong negative relationship with GDP per capita, plotted on a logarithmic axis. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 80,000 DALYs, while the horizontal axis spans from $2,000 to $100,000 in international dollars at 2021 prices. Circles are sized by population, with the largest representing over 1.4 billion people and the smallest around 600 million. Countries in sub-Saharan Africa occupy the upper-left quadrant with the highest disease burden and lowest income: the Central African Republic reaches approximately 80,000 DALYs per 100,000 at under $2,000 GDP per capita; Niger, Chad, Burundi, DR Congo, Mozambique, Mali, Zambia, Ethiopia, and Gambia all cluster between 50,000 and 70,000 DALYs at GDP levels below $5,000. In contrast, high-income nations in North America and Europe sit in the lower-right: the United States records around 30,000 DALYs at roughly $70,000 GDP; Germany and Japan fall near 20,000 DALYs at $50,000–$60,000 GDP; Singapore drops below 20,000 DALYs at over $100,000 GDP. Middle-income countries show intermediate positions: India and Indonesia hover around 30,000–40,000 DALYs at $5,000–$15,000 GDP; China sits near 20,000 DALYs at $20,000 GDP; Morocco and Vietnam lie between 25,000 and 35,000 DALYs at $5,000–$10,000 GDP. Some outliers break the general trend: Guyana and Greenland record higher GDPs ($40,000–$60,000) but maintain DALYs above 30,000; Fiji and Botswana show moderate GDPs ($10,000–$20,000) with DALYs near 40,000–50,000. The largest circles — India, China, Indonesia — anchor the middle of the distribution, while small-population states like Vanuatu, Jordan, and Poland occupy scattered positions across the range. The data shows that as GDP per capita increases along the logarithmic scale, DALYs per 100,000 consistently decline, with the steepest drop occurring between $2,000 and $20,000 GDP, after which the curve flattens toward lower disease burdens at higher incomes.
